title: rope_rescue_tower_bridge
description: A maintenance worker is suspended and unresponsive on a harness from a communications tower after a medical event.

timeline.title: Worker Unresponsive on Tower
timeline.description: Worker stopped moving at 40 m height. Coworker at top confirms unresponsive. Rescue team mobilised.

timeline.title: Rescue Rope Rigged
timeline.description: Rescue team rigs at tower top anchor. Rescuer begins descent toward suspended victim.

timeline.title: Victim Reached
timeline.description: Rescuer reaches victim at 40 m. Victim has suspension syndrome — pulseless at this point.

timeline.title: Emergency Lower Initiated
timeline.description: Rescuer attaches to victim and initiates emergency lower. Victim lowered to ground in 4 minutes.

timeline.title: Resuscitation at Ground
timeline.description: CPR initiated on ground. ROSC achieved. Victim transported to ICU.

node-1.title: Tower Worker Unresponsive
node-1.situation: A telecommunications worker at 40 metres on a lattice tower has stopped responding. A coworker at the top confirms no movement. The worker is suspended in a full-body harness. Wind is 30 km/h. A second coworker is attempting to climb down to the victim.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action as rescue team leader?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop the coworker from making an uncontrolled descent, dispatch a trained tower rescue team with rope systems, alert EMS for suspension trauma protocol, and begin rigging at the tower base.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow the coworker to descend — they know the tower and can reach the victim fastest.

options.label: C
options.text: Set up a ground-level aerial platform and extend it toward the victim.

node-2.title: Rigging the Rescue System
node-2.situation: The rescue team is at the tower top. The primary anchor is the tower's structural beam. The rescuer must descend on a controlled lower to the victim at 40 metres. A belay system is available. Wind gusts are affecting rope control.
node-2.question: How do you manage the descent safely?

options.label: A
options.text: Use a descender with a separate independent belay line managed by a second rescuer, monitor the rope for wind-induced swing, and descend steadily with communication to the ground team.

options.label: B
options.text: Descend as fast as possible on a single rope — speed is essential.

options.label: C
options.text: Clip onto the victim's anchor and free-climb down without a rescue rope.

node-3.title: Victim Assessment at Height
node-3.situation: The rescuer has reached the victim at 40 metres. The victim is unresponsive, pale, and has no palpable pulse. They have been hanging in a fully upright position for at least 20 minutes — consistent with suspension trauma/positional asphyxia.
node-3.question: What is the priority action at height?

options.label: A
options.text: Initiate an emergency lower immediately — CPR at height is impractical and ineffective. Get the victim to the ground as fast as safely possible.

options.label: B
options.text: Begin CPR at height while maintaining harness suspension.

options.label: C
options.text: Cut the victim free from the harness to allow a rapid lower without rope management.

node-4.title: Emergency Lower to Ground
node-4.situation: The rescuer has attached to the victim and initiated a tandem lower. The victim's dead weight combined with wind resistance is causing the descent device to overheat. Ground team is ready to receive the victim.
node-4.question: How do you manage the lowering safely?

options.label: A
options.text: Lower in short increments with brief pauses to dissipate heat from the descender, maintain verbal communications with ground team, and signal for immediate resuscitation upon landing.

options.label: B
options.text: Lower continuously at full speed — the victim is in cardiac arrest and every second counts.

node-5.title: Resuscitation at Ground
node-5.situation: The victim is on the ground. EMS confirms cardiac arrest. CPR is initiated. An AED has been attached and delivers one shock. After 90 seconds of CPR, a pulse is re-established (ROSC). The victim is breathing shallowly.
node-5.question: What is the post-ROSC priority?

options.label: A
options.text: Maintain airway, provide supplemental oxygen, keep the victim horizontal (not upright) to prevent recurrence of suspension syndrome, and transport urgently to an ICU-capable facility.

options.label: B
options.text: Sit the victim upright to help them breathe more easily — prone positioning is inappropriate.

node-6.title: Scenario Complete
node-6.situation: The victim was successfully lowered using a controlled rope rescue and received timely CPR and defibrillation. ROSC was achieved. Appropriate suspension trauma management (horizontal positioning post-rescue) was applied. The team's decision to prioritise rapid lowering over at-height CPR was correct.
